title: The Brooklyn Campus of Long Island University

infobox.value: is a [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in the borough of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York, United States. It is the original unit and first of two main campuses of the private [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University") system.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [The Brooklyn Campus of Long Island University](/w/index.php?title=The_Brooklyn_Campus_of_Long_Island_University&redirect=no "The Brooklyn Campus of Long Island University"))

Private university in New York City, U.S.

This article is about LIU Brooklyn. For the main campus, see [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University").

LIU Brooklyn

**LIU Brooklyn** is a [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in the borough of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York, United States. It is the original unit and first of two main campuses of the private [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University") system.

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Conolly_Residence_Hall%2C_LIU_Brooklyn%2C_Sept_2017.jpg/250px-Conolly_Residence_Hall%2C_LIU_Brooklyn%2C_Sept_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conolly_Residence_Hall,_LIU_Brooklyn,_Sept_2017.jpg)

Conolly Residence Hall

LIU Brooklyn is located at the intersection of [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") and [DeKalb Avenues](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue "DeKalb Avenue") (across the street from [Junior's](/wiki/Junior%27s "Junior's") restaurant and [City Point](/wiki/City_Point_(Brooklyn) "City Point (Brooklyn)")). The campus is served by the convergence of several [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") services at [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") ([B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)"), ​[N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)"), ​[R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains), [Nevins Street](/wiki/Nevins_Street_(IRT_Eastern_Parkway_Line) "Nevins Street (IRT Eastern Parkway Line)") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains), and [Jay Street–MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_(New_York_City_Subway) "Jay Street–MetroTech (New York City Subway)") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)")​, [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)")​​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains). The [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road")'s [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") is also nearby, as the [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") is located three blocks from campus.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The former [Brooklyn Paramount Theater](/wiki/Brooklyn_Paramount_Theater "Brooklyn Paramount Theater") was the world's first theater built specifically for talking pictures.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The theater, which abuts the original core campus, was bought in 1960 by LIU and converted into a gymnasium in 1963.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/LIU_Brooklyn_student_union_building%2C_Sept_2017.jpg/250px-LIU_Brooklyn_student_union_building%2C_Sept_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:LIU_Brooklyn_student_union_building,_Sept_2017.jpg)

Student union building

The first class at the campus' original site, located at 300 Pearl Street, had 312 students from the surrounding neighborhoods. The majority of students were immigrants or the children of immigrants, many of whom were the first in their families to ever attend college or school in general.[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3)

In 1929, the university affiliated itself with the Brooklyn College of Pharmacy, now known as LIU Pharmacy (the Arnold and Marie Schwartz College of Pharmacy and Health Sciences). In the late 1960s, plans were proposed by several trustees to sell the Brooklyn Campus in order to finance a new graduate campus on Long Island. Students and faculty held demonstrations protesting those plans. In 1972, administrators and faculty members negotiated the first collectively bargained faculty agreement at a private university in the United States.[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3) In the fall of 2016, the university locked out its faculty union and assigned administrators and replacement hires to teach classes.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The lockout resulted in a student walk out and protest and the loss of two weeks of semester time.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The LIU faculty responded by voting for ouster of the president, Kimberly Cline.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main articles: [LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn_Blackbirds "LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds") and [LIU Sharks](/wiki/LIU_Sharks "LIU Sharks")

Prior to the 2019–2020 school year, the Long Island University system unified its athletic programs and now all LIU athletic teams are referred to as the [LIU Sharks](/wiki/LIU_Sharks "LIU Sharks") and compete as a unified LIU athletic program.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Prior to 2019, LIU Brooklyn was the only unit of the LIU system to compete in Division I athletics and had 18 varsity teams. The school mascot was the Blackbirds.

In 1935–1936, the men's basketball team won all 25 of its games and was considered the top team in the country. With basketball becoming an Olympic sport, it seemed certain that the five starting players for the United States basketball team in the 1936 Summer Olympics in Berlin would be the five starters at LIU. However, because of the brutal anti-Jewish government in Germany, the team held a secret ballot and voted to not participate.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The men's basketball team won the [National Invitation Tournament](/wiki/National_Invitation_Tournament "National Invitation Tournament") (NIT) in 1939 and 1941 under the guidance of Coach [Clair Bee](/wiki/Clair_Bee "Clair Bee"). However, in 1951, the Blackbirds basketball players were involved in what's referred to as the [CCNY point-shaving scandal](/wiki/CCNY_point-shaving_scandal "CCNY point-shaving scandal") that resulted in five players receiving a suspended sentence, one player receiving a one-year prison sentence, and two former players receiving multi-year prison sentences for taking part in the scheme as fixers for the gamblers.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The basketball team was suspended for six years from 1951 to 1957.[[10]](#cite_note-NYDN-2009-01-06-10) Games were played at the [Brooklyn Paramount Theater](/wiki/Brooklyn_Paramount_Theater "Brooklyn Paramount Theater") until recently.[[10]](#cite_note-NYDN-2009-01-06-10)

In 1997, the Blackbirds were seeded 13th in the East Region of the NCAA men's basketball tournament. They lost in the first round to Villanova, 101–91. In 2011, LIU Brooklyn won both the [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") regular-season and tournament championship, winning 13 in a row at the end of the season. The Blackbirds were seeded 15th in the East Region of the [2011 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament](/wiki/2011_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "2011 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament") losing in the first round to second-seeded University of North Carolina.

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academics")]

* LIU Pharmacy (the [Arnold and Marie Schwartz College of Pharmacy and Health Sciences](/wiki/Arnold_and_Marie_Schwartz_College_of_Pharmacy "Arnold and Marie Schwartz College of Pharmacy")), founded in 1886
* The Harriet Rothkopf Heilbrunn School of Nursing
* The Richard L. Conolly College of Liberal Arts and Sciences
* The School of Business, Public Administration, and Information Sciences
* School of Education
* School of Health Professions
* School of Continuing Education
* [LIU Global](/wiki/LIU_Global "LIU Global")
* Honors College
* The TV Writer's Studio: a 2-year MFA in Writing and Producing for Television,[[11]](#cite_note-11) helmed by [Norman Steinberg](/wiki/Norman_Steinberg "Norman Steinberg"), cowriter of the film [Blazing Saddles](/wiki/Blazing_Saddles "Blazing Saddles").

## Ranking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ranking")]

For 2023, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranked LIU tied for #369 in National Universities.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LIU_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable alumni")]

* [Amaya Espinal](/wiki/Amaya_Espinal "Amaya Espinal") (born 1999), nurse, model and winner of [Love Island USA](/wiki/Love_Island_(American_TV_series)_season_7 "Love Island (American TV series) season 7").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Ossie Schectman](/wiki/Ossie_Schectman "Ossie Schectman") (1919–2013), basketball player who scored the first basket in National Basketball Association history
